papers.title: The Fastest Unbound Stars in the Universe
papers.author_names: James Guillochon (1) Abraham Loeb (1) ((1) Harvard ITC)

abstract.text: The discovery of hypervelocity stars (HVS) leaving our galaxy with speeds of nearly $10^{3}$ km s$^{-1}$ has provided strong evidence towards the existence of a massive compact object at the galaxy's center. HVS ejected via the disruption of stellar binaries can occasionally yield a star with $v_{\\\\infty} \\\\lesssim 10^4$ km s$^{-1}$, here we show that this mechanism can be extended to massive black hole (MBH) mergers, where the secondary star is replaced by a MBH with mass $M_2 \\\\gtrsim 10^5 M_{\\\\odot}$. We find that stars that are originally bound to the secondary MBH are frequently ejected with $v_{\\\\infty} > 10^4$ km s$^{-1}$, and occasionally with velocities $\\\\sim 10^5$ km s$^{-1}$ (one third the speed of light), for this reason we refer to stars ejected from these systems as "semi-relativistic" hypervelocity stars (SHS). Bound to no galaxy, the velocities of these stars are so great that they can cross a significant fraction of the observable universe in the time since their ejection (several Gpc). We demonstrate that if a significant fraction of MBH mergers undergo a phase in which their orbital eccentricity is $\\\\gtrsim 0.5$ and their periapse distance is tens of the primary's Schwarzschild radius, the space density of fast-moving ($v_{\\\\infty} > 10^{4}$ km s$^{-1}$) SHS may be as large as $10^{3}$ Mpc$^{-3}$. Hundreds of the SHS will be giant stars that could be detected by future all-sky infrared surveys such as WFIRST or Euclid and proper motion surveys such as LSST, with spectroscopic follow-up being possible with JWST.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [theoretical astrophysics research paper] The paper proposes a novel mechanism where eccentric mergers of binary massive black holes (BMBHs) eject tightly bound stars with semi-relativistic velocities (v∞ > 10⁴ km s⁻¹, up to ∼10⁵ km s⁻¹), forming 'semi-relativistic hypervelocity stars' (SHS). These stars are accelerated via dynamical scattering during close periapse passages, with ejection efficiency peaking when the secondary's periapse distance is tens of Schwarzschild radii and orbital eccentricity exceeds 0.5. Monte Carlo simulations show a space density of up to 10³ Mpc⁻³ for SHS, with hundreds of giant stars potentially detectable by future infrared and proper motion surveys like WFIRST, Euclid, LSST, and JWST—making SHS a new cosmological probe of BMBH merger dynamics.

source_fields.title: The Fastest Unbound Stars in the Universe

source_fields.sections_text: 3. Rates: This section explains that both the Hills mechanism and scattering by a random passer-by (including a merging MBH) produce high-velocity stars with similar velocity scaling and mass ejection patterns, while the removal of the secondary cluster's stars depends on the surrounding environment and power-law density distribution, determining the stalling radius for the secondary. 5. Results: Scattering experiments show that SHS production is rare (<1%) for small $\\tilde{a}$ due to tertiary destruction via tidal disruption, with destruction dominated by the secondary’s tidal radius rather than the primary’s IBCO, and SHS ejection efficiency increases with larger $\\tilde{a}$ where orbital changes are more likely to unbind the tertiary without destruction. 8. Discussion: The main experimental finding is that while photometric detection of stellar hypervelocity stars (SHS) is challenging due to sky confusion, SHS with velocities above a few thousand km s⁻¹ can be identified via high proper motions detectable by LSST, and their velocity vectors and trajectories—especially those beamed from BMBH mergers like M87—can point back to a common source galaxy, enabling identification of merger origins with velocity thresholds and spatial distributions. 9. Conclusions: This section concludes that hypervelocity stars (SHS/SHB) are unique tracers of eccentric supermassive black hole mergers, capable of traveling vast intergalactic distances and offering potential insights into both merger dynamics and cosmological expansion. 1. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism in which eccentric binary massive black hole mergers eject semi-relativistic hypervelocity stars from the secondary black hole via a Hills-like process, enabling the detection of stars with velocities exceeding $10^4$ km s$^{-1}$—a signature that would confirm eccentric BMBH mergers and serve as a new cosmological probe. 7. Observations: The observable population of unbound stars (SHS) is determined by modeling their initial velocities, escape energies from galaxy and nuclear clusters, and travel times, with detectable SHS reflecting the epoch of their ejection due to their long main-sequence lifetimes. 2. Speed Limits of MS Stars: The maximum velocities achievable for main-sequence stars in dynamical ejection events—such as the Hills mechanism or binary tidal disruption—are limited by the escape velocity of the least massive object and enhanced by the presence of a massive third body, with the Hills mechanism allowing for significantly higher ejection speeds than single- or two-body interactions. 4. BMBH Scattering Experiments: Monte Carlo scattering experiments at quadruple (and octuple) precision reveal that stellar ejections produce hypervelocity stars with typical velocities dependent on black hole merger rates and galaxy properties, with key results showing a strong dependence on bulge-to-total mass ratio and stellar mass scaling, and enabling precise back-reaction calculations on black holes with orbital energy and angular momentum errors below $10^{-14}$. 6. Semi-relativistic hypervelocity binaries: This section demonstrates that semi-relativistic hypervelocity binaries can be produced when a binary tertiary system survives tidal disruption in a triple stellar encounter, undergoing secular orbital evolution such as eccentric Kozai excitation before being ejected, with full four-body orbital dynamics explicitly simulated to conserve energy and angular momentum.

title: The Fastest Unbound Stars in the Universe

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper proposing ejection mechanism from binary MBH mergers, using Monte Carlo simulations to predict space densities and detectability by future surveys. No observational data or actual stellar objects presented.
